🚨 Critical Issues & Remediation

CRITICAL WLAN

Connection Failures

44 connection failure event(s): 42× Unspecified disconnection, 1× AP inactivity timeout, 1× Unauthenticated STA sent Class 2 / 3 frame

Failure Breakdown by Root Cause

Root CauseCount
Unspecified disconnection42
AP inactivity timeout1
Unauthenticated STA sent Class 2 / 3 frame1

🛠 Recommended Remediations per Root Cause

Root CauseRecommended Action
AP inactivity timeoutAP received no frames from the STA within the configured idle timer. Recovery: reduce AP idle-timeout threshold or enable null-data keep-alives on client. Check for power-save modes suppressing uplink traffic.
Unauthenticated STA sent Class 2 / 3 frameFrame received without a valid authentication or association context. Recovery: client must restart authentication from scratch (802.11 open auth → assoc → 4-way). Check for stale driver state after suspend/resume.
Unspecified disconnectionCheck AP and client syslogs/debug logs for context around disconnect time. Recovery: reconnect; enable verbose Wi-Fi logging to capture next occurrence.

🔑 EAPOL 4-way handshake — Msg1: 31 / Msg2: 1 / Msg3: 1 / Msg4: 2

📊 Validated Disconnect Reason:
AP inactivity timeout — client silent for 0s

Frame Evidence:

  • Last data frame: 0.3s before deauth/disassoc
  • AP inactivity timer fired — no uplink traffic received
  • EAPOL frames were in progress during idle period — check whether power-save mode suppressed data frames

🛠 Recommended Action:
Reduce AP idle-timeout or enable null-data keepalives on client. Disable deep power-save if EAPOL was concurrent.

🔑 EAPOL 4-way handshake — Msg1: 15 / Msg2: 0 / Msg3: 0 / Msg4: 0

Likely wrong PSK — EAPOL stall indicates MIC failure (code 1 = vendor fallback)

  • AP used unspecified code 1 instead of correct code 14/15

🛠 Recommended Action:
Enable verbose 802.11 logging on AP; verify passphrase and RSN config.

161346100:26:46.510AP→client🚨 DeauthenticationAP terminated connection — Class 2 frame received from non-authenticated STA
💡 Validated: Unauthenticated STA frame — class 2/3 violation (reason 6)		-27 dBm

🔑 EAPOL 4-way handshake — Msg1: 2 / Msg2: 1 / Msg3: 0 / Msg4: 0

📊 Validated Disconnect Reason:
Unauthenticated STA frame — class 2/3 violation (reason 6)

Frame Evidence:

  • Auth/Assoc did not complete before restricted frame was sent
  • Client transmitted a Class 2/3 frame outside authenticated or associated state

🛠 Recommended Action:
Reset client Wi-Fi driver; check for stale state after suspend/resume. Re-authenticate from scratch.

MEDIUM WLAN

High Retry Rate

High retry rate: 59.3% of data frames are retries (threshold: 15%)

What is this? A high percentage of wireless frames are being retransmitted, indicating RF interference, channel congestion, or hardware issues.

Impact: Degraded throughput, increased latency, and poor user experience on the wireless network.

🛠 Recommended Remediation

  1. Check for co-channel interference and adjust channel assignments
  2. Reduce AP transmit power to minimize overlap with neighboring APs
  3. Move to 5 GHz or 6 GHz bands to reduce 2.4 GHz congestion
  4. Inspect for faulty hardware (cables, antennas, APs)
MEDIUM WLAN

Action Frame Issues

1201 action frame(s) across 3 categories: 2 issue(s) found

📡 Action Frame Category Distribution

CategoryFrame Count
Radio Measurement (802.11k)1,128
Block Ack44
WNM (802.11v)29

📊 Action Frame Metrics

MetricCount
ADDBA Requests21
ADDBA Responses16
DELBA (Teardown)7
802.11v BTM Requests20
802.11v BTM Responses9

⚠ Detected Issues

MEDIUM WNM (802.11v) — Frequent BSS Transition Management steering

20 BTM Request(s) from AP(s) directing client(s) to roam. High BTM rate suggests aggressive steering — possibly due to AP overload, poor RF conditions, or band-steering policy.

Impact: Frequent roaming disrupts client connections; real-time applications (VoIP, video) may experience drops.

Steering APs:

  • c2:25:2f:7c:0c:87 — 20 BTM request(s)

Steered Clients:

  • 24:6a:0e:83:41:20 — 20 BTM request(s)

🛠 Fix: Review AP controller roaming/steering policy thresholds (RSSI, load). Check for RF coverage gaps causing ping-pong roaming. If band-steering: verify 5 GHz coverage is adequate before forcing clients off 2.4 GHz.

LOW WNM (802.11v) — BSS Transition rejected by clients

11 BTM Request(s) were not accepted by clients. Clients are refusing to roam to the suggested target AP.

🛠 Fix: Check if target AP is reachable with acceptable signal. Verify client supports 802.11v. Consider BTM with disassociation-imminent flag for mandatory steering.

MEDIUM WLAN

Control Frame Issues

Control frame analysis: 1 issue(s) — 14670 RTS, 11708 CTS, 0 PS-Poll, 201 BAR

📡 Control Frame Summary

Frame TypeCount
RTS Frames14,670
CTS Frames11,708
ACK Frames39,110
PS-Poll Frames0
Block Ack Requests (BAR)201
Block Ack Responses (BA)2,066
CF-End Frames0
RTS/CTS Ratio: 79.8% (11,708 CTS / 14,670 RTS) — ✅ Healthy

Top RTS Senders

DeviceRTS Frames
24:6a:0e:83:41:2014,342
c2:25:2f:7c:0c:87328

NAV/Duration: avg=227 μs, max=32,614 μs

⚠ Detected Issues

MEDIUM NAV/Duration — Excessive NAV duration values

134 frame(s) with NAV > 5ms (max: 32614 μs). Excessively large Duration/NAV values can starve other stations from accessing the medium (virtual carrier sense abuse).

Impact: Other stations defer transmission for the NAV duration, causing unfair medium access and throughput starvation.

Count: 134

Max NAV (μs): 32,614

Sources of Excessive NAV:

  • 24:6a:0e:83:41:20 — 22 frame(s)

🛠 Fix: Identify source of excessive NAV values (check TA field). Update driver/firmware. If intentional: possible NAV attack — enable AP-side protection.

MEDIUM WLAN

Power Save Issues

Power management analysis: 2 issue(s) — 42330 null frame(s), 29956 data frame(s)

42,330
Null / QoS-Null Frames
29,956
Real Data Frames
58.6%
Null-to-Data Ratio

👤 Per-Client Null Frame Activity

Client MACNull Frames Sent
24:6a:0e:83:41:2042,330

⚡ Power Management Transitions per Client

Client MACPM TransitionsFrames in PSFrames ActiveTotal Frames
24:6a:0e:83:41:2021,563 (32.8%)29,77336,03365,806
1a:78:7e:54:e1:ac6 (0.1%)38,1368,139

⚠ Detected Issues

MEDIUM Null Data — Null/QoS-Null frames dominate data traffic

Null/QoS-Null frames are 58.6% of all data-type frames (42330 null vs 29956 real data). Excessive null frames indicate clients are in power-save most of the time, spending more time on PS signaling than data transfer.

Impact: Network overhead from PS management; indicates idle or poorly-configured clients wasting airtime.

Null Frames: 42,330

Real Data Frames: 29,956

Null Ratio: 58.6%

🛠 Fix: Review client power-save configuration. Adjust DTIM interval on AP (longer DTIM = fewer wakeups). For active clients: disable aggressive power-save in driver.

LOW Power Management — Excessive PS transitions for 24:6a:0e:83:41:20

Client 24:6a:0e:83:41:20 had 21563 power-save mode transitions across 65806 frames (32.8% of frames are PS boundary changes). Rapid PS transitions cause latency spikes.

Impact: Each PS transition adds latency (50-200ms for buffered frames). Rapid cycling wastes battery and airtime.

Client: 24:6a:0e:83:41:20

PM Transitions: 21,563

Transition Rate: 32.8%

Transitions/sec: 2.1

🛠 Fix: Configure client to use WMM-PS (U-APSD) instead of legacy PS-Poll. Adjust PS wake interval in client driver. For latency-sensitive apps: disable power-save entirely.

📖 Null (0x0024) and QoS-Null (0x002c) frames carry no payload and are used for power-save signaling. The PM bit in the Frame Control field indicates whether the STA is entering (1) or exiting (0) power-save mode. Excessive PS cycling increases latency and wastes airtime. WMM-PS (U-APSD) is preferred over legacy PS-Poll for efficient buffered frame delivery.

Wpa3 Sae Failures

WPA3-SAE: 1 advisory notice(s) — WPA3-SAE + 4-way EAPOL handshake completed successfully (Msg1–Msg4 all seen, 5 S...

📝 Advisory Notices

WPA3-SAE + 4-way EAPOL handshake completed successfully (Msg1–Msg4 all seen, 5 SAE Confirm(s) with status=0).

📖 WPA3-SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) uses the Dragonfly key exchange (IEEE 802.11-2020 §12.4). Auth frames use algorithm=3 (SAE), seq=1 = SAE Commit, seq=2 = SAE Confirm. After SAE, a standard 4-way EAPOL/PTK handshake completes the session setup. Status codes 72–78 are SAE-specific.
